nativeRange.habitat: Coastal river systems and streams of southeastern Brazil; occurs in slower-moving sections of rivers, lakes, and reservoirs with soft sandy or silty substrates and variable vegetation; tolerates a wide temperature range from cool highland streams to warmer lowland waters

decorPreferences: deep fine sand substrate (minimum 5 cm) for sand-sifting behaviour
smooth rounded stones and slate for territory delineation
driftwood roots and branches for cover
robust or potted plants (Echinodorus, Vallisneria) — sand-sifting can uproot fine-rooted plants
open sandy foraging areas in the foreground

diet.notes: The Pearl Eartheater feeds by taking mouthfuls of substrate, extracting invertebrates, detritus, and organic matter, then expelling the sand through the gills — a behavior that plays out constantly throughout the day. In the aquarium it readily accepts high-quality cichlid pellets, sinking wafers, and a variety of frozen and live foods including bloodworm, earthworm, brine shrimp, and daphnia, along with blanched vegetables. A varied diet that includes some plant matter better reflects the species' omnivorous feeding in the wild; diets heavy in protein alone are generally best avoided. Feeding sinking foods helps prevent unnecessary competition at the water's surface.

goodWith: other robust South American cichlids of similar size (Satanoperca, Acarichthys, Cichla only in very large tanks)
large characins (silver dollars, giant danios) as dither fish
armoured catfish (large Loricariidae such as Pterygoplichthys, Hypostomus)
avoidWith: small tetras and nano fish (risk of predation or persecution)
delicate substrate plants — uprooted by constant sand-sifting
overly aggressive large cichlids (large Cichlasoma, Amphilophus) that will bully or injure

imageCandidates.notes: Cezary Porycki, 2768x1588. Tack-sharp studio profile of a vibrant adult showing the diagnostic pearl-spangled olive-gold flanks, black flank blotch, orange eye-ring and red/orange checkered fins.

Dnoerholm, 5560x3707. In-aquarium biotope shot with several individuals; brilliant blue iridescent pearl spotting across the flanks in a natural planted/driftwood setting - the display look hobbyists see.
Christoph.fr, 3648x2056. Sharp full-body side view of a single aquarium fish over sand showing the dark lateral band, pearl-spotted scales and red-margined fins.

summary: The Pearl Eartheater is a robust cichlid from the coastal river drainages of southeastern Brazil, where it sifts sandy substrates for invertebrates and organic matter. Known scientifically as Geophagus brasiliensis, it is widely regarded as one of the most cold-tolerant cichlids available to hobbyists, tolerating temperatures as low as 16 °C.
careNotes: Geophagus brasiliensis, commonly called the Pearl Eartheater, is the type species of the genus Geophagus — the original species that gives the group its name. A major taxonomic revision in the early 2000s transferred many species formerly grouped under Geophagus into related genera such as Gymnogeophagus, Mikrogeophagus, and Satanoperca, and G. brasiliensis remains one of the core species that stayed behind. Wild fish are found across coastal river drainages of southeastern Brazil, ranging from the state of Rio de Janeiro south to Rio Grande do Sul, where water temperatures can drop to around 16 °C in winter. This cold tolerance makes the Pearl Eartheater a viable option for unheated or lightly heated tanks in temperate climates, though keeping the species at the lower end of its range for extended periods is generally not recommended. Adults commonly reach 22–25 cm in the aquarium, with wild fish recorded up to 28 cm; a tank of at least 300 litres with a 150 cm footprint is the practical minimum for a pair. Fine sand at least 5 cm deep is essential — the species sifts substrate throughout the day and will redistribute the entire bottom decoration over time. Hardy plants such as Echinodorus or Vallisneria, kept in pots or anchored by large stones, hold up better than fine-rooted varieties that are easily dislodged. Water quality needs consistent attention, as large cichlids tend to be heavy feeders that produce significant waste; robust mechanical and biological filtration, typically a canister filter rated at four to six times the tank volume per hour, is widely recommended alongside regular partial water changes. A pH between 6.5 and 7.5 with moderate hardness suits the species well, though it tolerates a broader range within its natural parameters. Conspecific aggression is moderate in general, but breeding pairs tend to become significantly more territorial and may rearrange the tank around spawning time. Having a separate tank available for raising fry is advisable.
breedingNotes: The Pearl Eartheater is a larvophilous maternal mouthbrooder — meaning the female takes the hatched, free-swimming larvae into her mouth to protect them, rather than brooding the eggs themselves as some other substrate-spawning cichlids do. Eggs are deposited on a cleaned flat surface or in a pit excavated in the substrate, typically in clutches of 100 to 300 or more. Spawning is generally encouraged by a slight increase in temperature, large water changes, and a conditioning period on live or frozen foods. After hatching, the female broods the larvae for approximately 10 to 14 days; the male defends the surrounding territory during this period and tends to become noticeably aggressive toward other tank inhabitants. Fry are released as free-swimming juveniles and are immediately capable of accepting baby brine shrimp and finely crushed dry foods. For a short period after release, the female may still draw the fry back into her mouth when she perceives a threat. Breeding in the aquarium is achievable at an intermediate level of difficulty, but benefits from adequate space, pre-spawn conditioning, and, where possible, separating the pair from aggressive tankmates while spawning and brooding are underway.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Geophagus brasiliensis
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 28 cm TL), temperature range (16–28°C), pH (6.0–7.8), hardness, native range (SE Brazil coastal drainages), habitat, diet, sand-sifting behaviour, larvophilous mouthbrooding details, and compatibility guidance. Confirmed type-species status and genus revision context.

additional.name: FishBase — Geophagus brasiliensis
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 28 cm TL), temperature (16–28°C), native range (SE Brazil, Rio Grande do Sul to Rio de Janeiro and beyond), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), and diet notes. Confirmed broad temperature tolerance and omnivorous sand-sifting diet. Lifespan estimated 8–15 years based on cichlid longevity data for species of this size class.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Eartheater Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade care guide providing hobbyist-oriented guidance on tank size, substrate requirements (fine sand, 5+ cm depth), feeding, and compatibility. Confirmed intermediate care level, minimum tank size recommendations, and sand-sifting decor considerations.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Geophagus brasiliensis species profile
additional.notes: Provided supplementary information on conspecific aggression, breeding behaviour (larvophilous mouthbrooding), pair bonding, and temperature tolerance including cool-water hardiness. Confirmed semi-aggressive temperament and pair grouping recommendation.

additional.name: López-Fernández H & Taphorn DC (2004) — Geophaginae phylogeny and classification
additional.notes: Reference for the genus-level reclassification of former Geophagus species into Gymnogeophagus, Mikrogeophagus, Satanoperca, and allied genera. Confirms G. brasiliensis as the type species remaining in Geophagus sensu stricto. Referenced via FishBase nomenclature records.

fish.breedingNotes: The species is a larvophilous maternal mouthbrooder, meaning the female broods the free-swimming larvae orally for approximately 10 to 14 days after hatching rather than incubating the eggs. Clutches typically range from 100 to 300 or more eggs, laid on cleaned flat surfaces or in substrate pits. Breeding pairs tend to become highly territorial during spawning; adequate space and careful tankmate management are important for success. Fry are capable of accepting baby brine shrimp and micro-crushed foods immediately upon release.
